channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Senate Ruling Threatens Ballroom Funding in G.O.P. Budget Bill
item.description: Democrats announced Saturday night that the Senate’s top parliamentary referee had determined that the $1 billion provision did not comply with budget rules.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.category: White House East Wing and Ballroom (Washington, DC)
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: Crews continued work on the White House ballroom on Friday.

item.title: Shoppers’ Frenzy for ‘Royal Pop’ Pocket Watches Forces Swatch to Shut Stores
item.description: The pocket watches, a collaboration with the luxury timepiece maker Audemars Piguet, drew crowds to stores and malls around the world.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ziegler
item.media:description: Customers had been waiting outside a Swatch store in Geneva. The company closed dozens of its stores on Saturday as crowds lined up to snag its new pocket watch, a collaboration with Audemars Piguet.

item.title: China Indicates Tariffs Were Discussed at Trump Summit
item.description: China’s Ministry of Commerce said Saturday that the countries had struck a preliminary agreement to reduce some tariffs, seemingly contradicting statements by President Trump
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:description: President Trump said Friday that he had not discussed U.S. tariffs on imports from China, a key issue going into the summit with China’s leader, Xi Jinping.

item.title: Thousands of FiveThirtyEight Articles Seemingly Vanish From the Internet
item.description: The influential polling analysis site was shut down last year, but an earlier archived version, fivethirtyeight.com, had lived on. Now the site is redirecting users to ABC News.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald
item.media:description: Nate Silver, center, in 2015. Mr. Silver founded FiveThirtyEight as a blog in 2008 before building it into a highly influential polling analysis operation.

item.title: Oysters, Champagne and Billionaires Buying Art
item.description: When the European Fine Art Foundation alights in Manhattan, it’s something like a billionaire version of the classic television game show “Supermarket Sweep.”
item.dc:creator: Guy Trebay

item.title: U.S. Debt Is Now Bigger Than the Economy. That’s Not the Real Problem.
item.description: Washington quickly brushed off a startling milestone in a mounting problem.
item.dc:creator: Peter Coy

item.title: Which Countries Are Profiting From the Iran War Oil Shock
item.description: An analysis of oil export data offers clues about which nations have benefited from higher prices, and which have lost a lot of revenue.
item.dc:creator: Amy Fan and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Josh Tyrangiel book excerpt: How OpenAI and Khan Academy Made a Chatbot
item.description: Inside a collaboration to bring artificial intelligence into the classroom.
item.dc:creator: Josh Tyrangiel
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by Pablo Delcan and Danielle Del Plato

item.title: Political Money Is Flowing to Influencers. But From Whom?
item.description: Social media stars have become a magnet for campaigns and political groups that want to push priorities without disclosing where their money is going.
item.dc:creator: Ken Bensinger
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times

item.title: NPR Podcast Host Ramtin Arablouei Exits Amid Workplace Inquiry
item.description: Ramtin Arablouei, a co-host of “Throughline,” left the network after an employee made a human resources complaint about his behavior.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: NPR studios in Washington, D.C.

item.title: Trump Administration Weighs $1.7 Billion Fund for Allies Investigated Under Biden
item.description: Critics denounced the highly unusual plan, which has yet to be finalized or approved, as a vast political slush fund financed by taxpayers.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush, Andrew Duehren and Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Stefani Reynolds for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump controls both the I.R.S., the agency he sued, and the Justice Department, which would typically represent the I.R.S. against legal claims.

item.title: Stock Market Rally Confronts Inflation and Higher Interest Rates
item.description: Despite accelerating inflation and possible interest rate increases, the S&P 500 has posted a long weekly winning streak, driven by strong corporate earnings. Can it last?
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison

item.title: For Xi’s Critics, Trump’s China Visit Is Fuel for Jokes They Can’t Tell
item.description: On Threads and other sites, liberal-minded Chinese accounts were mocking the proceedings and offering a rare window into opinions on Xi Jinping and his leadership style.
item.dc:creator: Li Yuan
item.media:description: President Trump was cheered by children upon his arrival in Beijing on Thursday

item.title: Some Medicare Patients Can Now Get Free CBD
item.description: The Trump administration has authorized a test program to see if the cannabis compound can ease some symptoms and reduce health care costs among older patients.
item.dc:creator: Jan Hoffman
item.category: Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services
item.media:credit: Jackie Russo for The New York Times
item.media:description: CBD products are already popular with some Medicare-age patients.

item.title: Starbucks to Layoff 300 Corporate Workers
item.description: The company said it would close four regional offices and take a $400 million charge related to the changes.
item.dc:creator: Julie Creswell
item.media:description: The Seattle headquarters of Starbucks, which said it would close regional offices in Atlanta, Chicago, Dallas and Burbank, Calif.

item.title: ‘No Essay’ College Scholarships May Have Unseen Strings Attached
item.description: Unlike traditional financial aid, the prizes are awarded by random drawings. The websites, in turn, gain access to applicants’ personal information.
item.dc:creator: Ann Carrns

item.title: What Trump Gained, and Didn’t, From China
item.description: President Trump is returning home from Beijing after a two-day summit that achieved a few deals but left investors and analysts underwhelmed.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Ian Mount and Grady McGregor
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The two-day summit between President Trump and Xi Jinping, China’s leader, has wrapped up with big questions over what they accomplished.

item.title: Oil Prices Rise as Trump-Xi Summit Yields No Clear Breakthroughs on Iran War
item.description: Hopes for an end to the war in Iran faded after President Trump failed to secure a commitment from China to help persuade Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Elon Musk’s Awkward Selfie Moment With a Chinese Billionaire in Beijing
item.description: A few seconds of selfie awkwardness between Elon Musk and Lei Jun, a prominent Chinese entrepreneur, has spread like wildfire on China’s social media.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Murphy Zhao

item.title: Trump Touts ‘Fantastic Trade Deals’ With China, but Details Are Scarce
item.description: The president left Beijing following a summit with China, during which the two countries sought to stabilize their economic and political relations.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:description: President Trump before departing China in Beijing on Friday.

item.title: Medicare Coverage for GLP-1 Weight Loss Drugs: Cost, Eligibility and What to Know
item.description: What you should know about the federal government’s pilot program offering GLP-1s solely for weight loss.
item.dc:creator: Mark Miller
item.media:description: The list of GLP-1s that can be prescribed for obesity includes Eli Lilly’s Foundayo, Wegovy in injectable and tablet forms, and Eli Lilly’s Zepbound KwikPen.

item.title: As Powell Steps Down, the Fed Confronts ‘Regime Change’
item.description: Jerome H. Powell is passing the chair’s baton to Kevin M. Warsh at the Federal Reserve, an institution that President Trump’s pick says needs an overhaul.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times, left; Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times

item.title: Joe Sedelmaier, Auteur Behind ‘Where’s the Beef?’ Ad, Dies at 92
item.description: He directed nearly 1,000 comedic commercials, including a much-quoted spot for Wendy’s and one for FedEx featuring a manic speed talker.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:description: Clara Peller, the octogenarian star of a classic Wendy’s commercial directed by Joe Sedelmaier, asks her famous question during a news conference in 1984.

item.title: It’s Not Just U.S. Stocks. A.I. and Oil Are Moving Global Markets, Too.
item.description: Intel, along with Taiwan and South Korea, are the latest winners, our columnist says. True diversification is hard to find.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Sommer
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Only a year ago, Intel needed a bailout by the U.S. government. This year, it has made a remarkable comeback.

item.title: Nvidia’s Future in China Remains Unclear After Trump-Xi Summit
item.description: The standoff comes as Chinese firms increasingly turn to domestic chipmakers like Huawei, in a drive to reduce China’s dependence on Western technologies.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin
item.media:description: Jensen Huang, Nvidia’s chief executive, after a welcome ceremony at the Great Hall of the People in Beijing on Thursday.

item.title: Trump and Boeing Announce China Will Purchase 200 Planes
item.description: The deal, which Beijing has not commented on, would be a major win for Boeing, which has lost ground to Airbus in one of the world’s largest aviation markets.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher, Qasim Nauman and Niraj Chokshi
item.media:description: Chinese airlines are some of Boeing’s biggest customers.

item.title: Spies, Sanctions, Cyberattacks: China and the U.S. Clash Behind the Scenes
item.description: After months of avoiding confrontation, the Trump administration has taken recent steps to call out China on Iran, artificial intelligence and spying.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger and Dustin Volz
item.media:description: President Trump met with President Xi Jinping of China on Thursday after a flurry of actions from his administration in recent weeks against China.

item.title: Cerebras, A.I. Chip Maker, Rises 89% in Market Debut as Tech IPOs Ramp Up
item.description: Cerebras, a Silicon Valley maker of artificial intelligence chips, began trading on the stock market on Thursday, as SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic also take steps to go public.
item.dc:creator: Natallie Rocha
item.media:credit: Christie Hemm Klok for The New York Times
item.media:description: Cerebras presages a series of potential “mega I.P.O.s” from A.I.-related firms including OpenAI, Anthropic and Elon Musk’s SpaceX.

item.title: Consumers Spent More in April Despite High Gas Prices
item.description: Retail sales rose 0.5 percent despite higher prices for gas, food and other goods. But there are signs consumers are under some strain.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Retail sales data on Thursday showed American consumers are continuing to spend even as gas prices go up and paychecks are not keeping up with inflation.

item.title: Trump Might Welcome Chinese Investment, but America Is Wary
item.description: A pledge for more Chinese investment could face backlash given longstanding national security concerns in the United States.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.media:description: President Trump on Thursday in Beijing. There has been speculation that Mr. Trump and his advisers are seeking to secure a major investment pledge from China on the trip.

item.title: The Met Will Expand by Merging With the Nearby Neue Galerie
item.description: Beginning in 2028, the Metropolitan Museum of Art will own the Neue’s Fifth Avenue home and the prestige collection of 20th-century Austrian and German art built by Ronald S. Lauder.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin

item.title: A Fact Check of Trump’s Claims on Inflation and Gas Prices in Iran War
item.description: He has minimized soaring gas prices, rising inflation and the American economy’s need for the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Linda Qiu
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Gabriel V. Cárdenas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gas prices have risen about 53 percent higher than they were at the start of the war on Feb. 28.

item.title: In Qatar, Energy Sector Damage Is Severe, and the Way Back Will Be Long
item.description: Iranian strikes and a blockade have paralyzed Qatar’s gas engine, creating a technical bottleneck likely to stall exports for years.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A liquefied natural gas production facility in Qatar’s Ras Laffan Industrial City. Qatar is one of the world’s largest exporters of L.N.G., but Iranian attacks have crippled its production.

item.title: Dan Loeb, Bobby Kotick and Other Business Leaders Backing Spencer Pratt
item.description: The former reality-TV star has shaken up the Los Angeles mayoral race, gaining support from deep-pocketed donors like the financier Daniel S. Loeb.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly and Brian O’Keefe
item.media:description: Spencer Pratt, the former reality-TV star, is shaking up Los Angeles politics with a mayoral run that’s attracting some deep-pocketed donors.

item.title: Oil Prices Waver as Trump Meets China’s Xi to Discuss Strait of Hormuz
item.description: The president is expected to urge the Chinese leader to help ease tensions in the Middle East by helping to persuade Iran to end the war.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Can Some Very Tiny Particles Cool the Planet? One Tech Company Says Yes.
item.description: Stardust Solutions says its tiny spheres can reflect the sun’s rays without harming people or the environment. Critics say private companies have no business altering Earth’s atmosphere.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.media:credit: Avishag Shaar-Yashuv for The New York Times

item.title: Conservative Influencers Tap a Nonprofit to Pay for Their Security
item.description: A charity is raising money to provide security, arguing that protecting some of right-wing media’s biggest stars is a public good.
item.dc:creator: Ken Bensinger
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nick Shirley, a right-wing YouTuber and influencer, left, at the Conservative Political Action Conference in Grapevine, Texas, in March.

item.title: Crypto Industry Pushes a Bill to Tilt Regulation in Its Favor
item.description: After a series of political victories under President Trump, firms are lobbying Congress for a sweeping framework they helped shape.
item.dc:creator: David Yaffe-Bellany

item.title: Honda Posts First-Ever Annual Loss After Pullback From E.V.s
item.description: The Japanese automaker posted its first loss since 1957 as it took a multibillion-dollar hit from scaling back its electric-vehicle plans.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis
item.media:description: A Honda dealership in Osaka, Japan.

item.title: Mamdani Urges State to Block Western Union’s Deal for Intermex
item.description: New York City’s mayor said the company’s proposed acquisition of Intermex could make it costlier for immigrants to send money abroad.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch
item.category: Intermex Wire Transfer LLC (International Money Express)
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani of New York sent a state agency a letter asking it to block Western Union’s proposed $500 million acquisition of Intermex.

item.title: Trump Administration Will Withhold $1.3 Billion in Medicaid Payments to California
item.description: Vice President JD Vance said the state had not done enough to fight fraud in the public health insurance program.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff
item.category: Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services
item.media:description: Vice President JD Vance said that California had failed to combat fraud in Medicaid.

item.title: What to Watch at Trump and Xi’s U.S.-China Summit
item.description: Here’s what to know about the biggest sources of tension in U.S.-China relations before the first summit in Beijing in nine years between the nations’ leaders.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport, Ana Swanson and Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump arrived in Beijing on Wednesday.

item.title: Senate Confirms Kevin Warsh as Fed Chair
item.description: President Trump’s pick to replace Jerome H. Powell takes the helm at a highly uncertain moment for the economy and with heightened concern about the Federal Reserve’s ability to operate independently.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:description: Kevin M. Warsh has vowed to embark on “regime change” at the Federal Reserve.

item.title: Eric Trump, Who Runs the Family Business, Hops Along on the Plane to China
item.description: The president’s middle son is said to be joining his father in a “personal capacity” rather than to make business deals.
item.dc:creator: Ben Protess
item.media:description: President Trump and his delegation, including his son Eric Trump, at the Beijing Capital Airport on Wednesday.

item.title: Trump Administration Pushes I.R.S. to Identify Undocumented Immigrants
item.description: The deliberations are a sign that the Trump administration may once again try to harness the power of the I.R.S. to advance its immigration agenda.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren and Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: A tax preparation clinic in Los Angeles. The Internal Revenue Service has in effect looked the other way on immigration status, focusing instead on encouraging people to pay the taxes that they may owe.

item.title: Fatih Birol of the International Energy Agency: What It Will Take to End the Energy Crisis
item.description: Fatih Birol of the International Energy Agency, who orchestrated a multinational release of oil reserves, detailed the risks facing the economy now and beyond.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: “The single most important solution to this problem today is fully and unconditionally opening up of the strait,” Fatih Birol, who leads the International Energy Agency, said in an interview this week.

item.title: Rich Danker, RFK Jr.’s Top Spokesman, Resigns in Protest Over New Vaping Policy
item.description: In a letter to President Trump, the spokesman, Rich Danker, said allowing the sale of flavored e-cigarettes would enhance their appeal to children.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.media:credit: Michael A. McCoy for The New York Times
item.media:description: The resignation comes at a fraught time for the Department of Health and Human Services, which now has three major vacancies.

item.title: Why the Bombing of Iran Tied the U.S. More Closely to China
item.description: As the U.S. tries to rebuild its weapons stockpiles drained in the Iran war, it will need access to rare-earth minerals, an industry China dominates.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and John Ismay
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A destroyed building in Tehran last month. The war in Iran is expected to figure into talks between the United States and China in Beijing this week.
